coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and graphene oxide based FRET immunosensor for sensitive detection of Helicobacter pylori
coredata.dc:creator: Chattopadhyay, Sruti
Choudhary, Meenakshi
Singh, Harpal
coredata.dc:description: We report a novel Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) immunosensor for sensitive detection of whole cell H. pylori, a causative organism for gastric carcinoma. Highly fluorescent and well-dispersed functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) were synthesized and chemically conjugated with anti-H. pylori antibody to fabricate a fluorescent probe (FCDs-Ab). The fluorescence of FCDs-Ab gets quenched upon interaction with graphene oxide (GO), whereas in presence of H. pylori, the interaction between the FCD-Ab and GO gets interrupted and gradual restoration of fluorescence was observed due to the specific affinity and binding of Ab towards H. pylori. The immunosensor was characterized at each step of fabrication. The assay exhibits a linear detection range of 5-107 cells mL−1 with limit of detection (LOD) of 10 cells mL−1 with high specificity and selectivity to target pathogen. The analytical performance of the developed immunosensor was evaluated with different spiked food samples and the substantial recovery validates its potential for risk assessment in food testing, thus ensuring general safety of public health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FRET
Graphene oxide
Functionalized carbon dots
Whole cell pathogen
Immunosensor
Helicobacter pylori

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) and graphene oxide (GO)
Bio-conjugation of FCDs with anti-H. pylori antibody
Concentration and time optimization of GO with FCD-ab
Quantitative analysis of H. pylori in PBS and real food samples
Detection of H. pylori by ELISA
Synthesis and characterisation of FCDs and GO
Morphological analysis of GO and FCD-Ab-GO complex
Working principle of FRET based immunosensor
Specificity and selectivity study of the FRET immunosensor
Detection of H. pylori using sandwich ELISA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images of FCDs. Inset: Line and arrows indicate the lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.327 nm (b) AFM image, inset: corresponding height profile of FCDs (c) particle size distribution of FCDs (size 3.79 nm and PDI 0.204) and (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of FCDs, (e) fluorescence emission spectra of FCDs at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 380 nm, (f) ATR-FTIR spectrum and (g) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GO.

(a) ATR-FTIR spectra of FCDs (i) before and (ii) after attachment of anti-Helicobacter pylori antibody, (b) fluorescence spectra of FCDs and FCD-Ab conjugates, (c) fluorescence spectra of FCD-Ab conjugates in presence of various concentration of GO ranging from 0 to 60 μg mL−1, (d) Stern-Volmer plot of quenching system, FCD-Ab-GO. FESEM images of (e) GO and (f) FCD-Ab-GO.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of FCD-Ab-GO in presence of different concentrations of H. pylori varying from 0-107 cells mL−1, (b) the corresponding dose-response curve, (c) statistical analysis of fluorescence data at various pathogen concentrations to deduce level of significance (ns: p > 0.05, **p ≤ 0.01, ****p ≤ 0.0001) and (d) FESEM image of FCD-Ab-Pylori assembly.
(a) Specificity and selectivity studies for the detection of H. pylori (****p ≤ 0.0001) and (b) effect of sample matrix: H. pylori spiked in various food samples (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of FCD-Ab-GO based FRET immunosensor for whole cell H. pylori detection.
Comparative table of various reported sensors for detection of H. pylori.
Recovery study of H. pylori in real samples using FRET immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and graphene oxide based FRET immunosensor for sensitive detection of Helicobacter pylori

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, N.D.-16, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, N.D.-16, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi, 110029, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: All India Institute of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi 110029, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016, India.
Corresponding author. Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a novel Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) immunosensor for sensitive detection of whole cell H. pylori, a causative organism for gastric carcinoma. Highly fluorescent and well-dispersed functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) were synthesized and chemically conjugated with anti-H. pylori antibody to fabricate a fluorescent probe (FCDs-Ab). The fluorescence of FCDs-Ab gets quenched upon interaction with graphene oxide (GO), whereas in presence of H. pylori, the interaction between the FCD-Ab and GO gets interrupted and gradual restoration of fluorescence was observed due to the specific affinity and binding of Ab towards H. pylori. The immunosensor was characterized at each step of fabrication. The assay exhibits a linear detection range of 5-107 cells mL−1 with limit of detection (LOD) of 10 cells mL−1 with high specificity and selectivity to target pathogen. The analytical performance of the developed immunosensor was evaluated with different spiked food samples and the substantial recovery validates its potential for risk assessment in food testing, thus ensuring general safety of public health.

ce:section.ce:para: Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) is highly pathogenic and widespread bacteria in human population with almost about half the earth population considered as its host [1,2]. It infects the human gastrointestinal tract causing permanent damage and may lead to most common chronic clinical disorders of the upper gastrointestinal tract such as chronic-active gastritis, duodenal and gastric ulcer disease that ultimately lead to stomach cancer if not cured on time [2]. World Health Organization (WHO) classifies H. pylori as a class I carcinogen that persistently colonizes the stomach lining to cause gastric carcinoma, gastritis, peptic and duodenal ulcer, gastric lymphoma and liver cancer [3,4]. Although the exact routes of H. pylori transmission still not fully investigated, the most commonly accepted hypothesis is that infection takes place through the faecal-oral route via contaminated water and foods i.e. environmental transmission [5]. Moreover, literature reports suggested that the presence and survival of H. pylori in various food products such as ready to eat salads, meat, vegetables, and milk can play a major role in spreading of infection [6]. Apart from faecal oral route some studies have also reported the iatrogenic (through medical procedures like dental and endoscopic) transmission [7] and transmission through oro-oral route - H. pylori infected person can also transmit the infection to other close contacts i.e. interpersonal transmission, through kissing, eating food from same plate as pathogen can be found in saliva and plaque on the teeth. Therefore, infections tend to cluster in families and among people who live in nursing homes and other supervised facilities [8,9]. The incidence rates of H. pylori infection are much higher in developing countries (in some areas >85%) than in Europe and North America (∼30%–40%) due to unhygienic living conditions, poor sanitation, poor socioeconomic status [10,11]. Moreover, the routine medical treatment for H. pylori infection is becoming a challenge due to its increasing antibiotic resistance [8]. Keeping in view of the above-mentioned facts, there is an utmost need of in vitro detection of H. pylori to monitor the infections at very early onset and in contaminated food and water [12,13]. The available approaches and techniques to detect H. pylori in vitro are by electrochemical and colorimetric sensors, Enzyme Linked Sorbent Assays (ELISA), Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) and Lateral Flow Devices [1,14]. At the lab scale, H. pylori diagnosis involves molecular methods like ELISA [15] and PCR [3] however, these methods are found to be complicated, laborious and time intensive. Therefore, a quick detection method which can facilitate selectivity, sensitivity and good specificity in complex samples is still needed. In this concern, Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) based biosensing may be the possible answer which rely on the optical energy transfer phenomena from donor to acceptor (quencher) [16].

Nowadays, carbon-based nanomaterials are of great interest in the FRET based biosensing. Recently, carbon nanodots (C-dots) and carbon nanocrystals have been used to develop fluorescence based sensitive methods owing to their great photostability, broad excitations and narrow emissions. Apart from that, they exhibit excellent fluorescent property, water dispersibility, low toxicity and good biocompatibility due to which they are being used as an alternative candidate to synthetic organic dyes, as a fluorescence probe in drug and gene delivery, detection of metal ions and pathogenic microorganisms, in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging and detection of organic molecules [17–19]. Highly fluorescent and biocompatible C-dots were synthesized by Chattopadhyay et al., 2021, for the detection of metal ions and their removal from cells through chelation via exocytosis [18].

Graphene oxide (GO) which is a two-dimensional carbon material with pendant functional groups like –COOH, –OH, and –CO etc. Exhibit good physicochemical properties like high water dispersibility, facile surface modification to achieve high biocompatibility [20]. Apart from that, GO also possess unique and interesting structural, mechanical, thermal and electronic properties due to which it is a material of interest as a new energy acceptor in energy transfer reactions and photoluminescence-based sensors [21,22]. The sp2 aromatic domains present in GO can induce efficient fluorescence quenching either via FRET or non-radiative dipole-dipole coupling, make it an ideal candidate for fluorescence turn-on sensing [22]. Lu et al. have reported the quenching of fluorescence energy by GO (acceptor) from organic dye molecules which act as donor in excited state resulting in the phenomenon of FRET [23]. Recently, inorganic [16,22] as well as organic quantum dots [18,24,25] has been applied for the detection of metal ions and biomolecules. However, FRET based detection of H. pylori using combination of C-dots and GO has not been investigated yet.

In the present study, a FRET based immunosensor is aimed for the quantitative detection of H. pylori using anti-H. pylori antibody modified functionalized carbon dots (FCD-Ab) as a fluorophore and GO as a quencher (Scheme 1 ). The immunosensor was characterized by Attenuated Total Reflection-Fourier Transform Infra-Red (ATR-FTIR), fluorescence spectroscopy and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) to substantiate the bioconjugation of FCDs with Ab (FCD-Ab) and their subsequent interaction with GO (FCD-Ab-GO). Specificity and selectivity of the assay were studied with various dissimilar pathogens. The performance of the developed immunosensor was also compared with conventional ELISA based assay. Furthermore, the practical applicability of developed immunosensor was eventually analyzed on deliberately spiked samples such as lettuce, fenugreek leaves, milk, and minced meat to validates its potential for risk assessment in real food. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report to use FCD-Ab-GO fluorescence quenching complex for the detection of whole cell H. pylori. Thus, the developed FRET based immunosensor can be considered for ensuring food safety.

Citric acid (CA), tris(hydroxylmethyl)aminomethane (Tris), buffer reagents were purchased from Merck, India. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Graphite powder (99.9%, 325 mesh) was purchased from Alfa Aesar, Germany. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and sodium nitrate (NaNO3) were purchased from Merck, India. In this study all chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Lettuce, fenugreek leaves, minced meat and low-fat milk were supplied by local market. Culture of 109 CFU mL−1 concentration of heat killed bacteria H. pylori and 1 mg mL−1 of affinity purified polyclonal anti-Helicobacter pylori antibody and 0.1 mg peroxidase-labelled antibody to H. pylori were purchased from Kirkegaard-Perry Laboratories (KPL), Inc. (Gaithersburg, USA) through Averon Life Sciences (New Delhi, India). ELISA substrate 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Bangalore Genie (Bangalore, India). Some cross-reacting species of Helicobacter pylori including Escherichia coli, Shigella sonnei, Staphylococcus aureus were brought from Religare Ranbaxy Pharmaceuticals (Gurgaon, India) and Salmonella typhimurium was from National Institute of Immunology, Delhi, India. All buffer solutions, GO and FCDs sample were prepared in De-ionized MilliQ water (18Ω, Millipore, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) and graphene oxide (GO)
ce:section.ce:para: FCDs were synthesized in microwave from citric acid and trace amounts of Tris. Briefly, CA and Tris in molar ratio of 1:0.4 were added to 20 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 10 min and then transferred into a domestic microwave oven (700W) and intermittently heated for 5 min at medium heat [26]. Finally, the product was dispersed in deionized water and centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles. The supernatant was collected and further purified by 0.22 μm syringe filter. FCDs dispersion were finally purified by dialysis against deionized water using a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 24 h and stored in a cool, dark place till further use. Pale orange coloured solution of FCDs was obtained which was characterized by several analytical techniques. GO was prepared by modified Hummer's method [20]. 1 g graphite powder and 1 g NaNO3 were dissolved in H2SO4 in an ice bath with continuous stirring. 6 g of KMnO4 was slowly added to the above solution under stirring. After half an hour, this solution was then transferred to water bath at room temperature and kept on vigorous stirring for 1 h to make a thick paste. After that, 100 mL of double distilled water was added, and the solution was kept on stirring for 30 min in hot water bath at 90 ± 5 °C. To this solution, 200 mL of double distilled water was added followed by the slow addition of 6 mL of H2O2 (30%). The warm solution was then filtered and washed with 200 mL of water. The filtered cake was then redispersed in water by mechanical agitation and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Washing and centrifugation steps were repeated until the supernatant attains neutral pH. The final pellet was redispersed in water by ultra-sonication to obtain solution of exfoliated GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-conjugation of FCDs with anti-H. pylori antibody
ce:section.ce:para: Attachment of biomolecules on FCDs was achieved using pathogen specific antibody via EDC/NHS coupling. 50 μg of FCDs was dispersed in 1 mL of deionized water followed by addition of 30 μL EDC (400 mM) and 30 μL NHS (100 mM). The reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min and then 5 μg of polyclonal anti-H. pylori antibody was added and stirring was continued for another 2 h at 25 °C in dark. Finally, the solution was kept at 4 °C for overnight to ensure the complete covalent binding of antibody on to the surface of activated FCDs. Biofunctionalized FCDs (FCD-Ab) was purified by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 8 min via filtration membrane (1000 Da) to remove unreacted reagents. The FCD-Ab conjugates on the membrane were suspended in a 0.01 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C till further use. Bioconjugation of FCDs was also characterized by ATR-FTIR and fluorescence studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration and time optimization of GO with FCD-ab
ce:section.ce:para:
Various concentrations of GO suspensions (0–60 μg) were mixed with a fixed concentration of 10 μg of FCD-Ab in 1 mL of 0.01 M PBS and incubated for different time intervals at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded under the excitation at 330 nm. The optimum concentration and incubation time for best interaction of GO and FCD-Ab were identified. The quenching efficiency of GO was determined by using Stern-Volmer equation-
(1) F0/F = kq[C] + 1------------
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of FCD-Ab conjugate in the absence and presence of GO respectively. Kq is the slope of the linear fit and [C] is the concentration of GO.
Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) was used to analyse the morphology of GO before and after the interaction with FCD-Ab.

All strains of bacteria (Escherichia coli, Shigella sonnei, Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella typhimurium) were isolated under strictly maintained conditions and used after proper heat inactivation. Briefly, 100 μL of bacterial solution was plated on sterilized agar plate and spread homogenously. After 24 h of culture at 37 °C, single colony of bacteria was isolated to avoid any co-contaminant and added in broth medium and allowed to incubate for 24 h for substantial multiplication. These bacteria were heat killed via thermal shock by maintaining the temperature at 80 °C for 30 min. Concentration of bacterial suspension was adjusted to 2 × 108 cells/mL by spectrophotometer at 595 nm and diluted serially to prepare a range of concentrations of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of H. pylori in PBS and real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μg of FCD-Ab was incubated with 50 μg mL−1 of GO suspension for 20 min. Bacterial culture of H. pylori ranging from 5 to 107 cells were added to this quenched system (FCD-Ab-GO) with gentle shaking. The total volume of each solution was adjusted to 1 mL with 0.01 M PBS buffer and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. One tube was prepared without adding bacteria as negative control. Fluorescence spectra of these solutions were recorded at 330 nm to measure the fluorescence recovery of FCDs.

To evaluate the specificity of this proposed FRET immunosensor, heat inactivated samples of Escherichia coli, Shigella sonnei, Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella typhimurium were incubated separately with the above-mentioned quenched system (FCD-Ab-GO) with final concentration of 107 cells/mL and then the fluorescence intensity were measured using the same protocol used for the detection of H. pylori.

Recovery studies were carried out using lettuce, fenugreek leaves, minced meat and low-fat milk as realistic solid and liquid food samples, respectively. 50 g of finely chopped lettuce, fenugreek leaves and grinded minced meat were separately shaken with 250 mL of autoclaved 0.01 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The supernatant was filtered after discarding the solid residue. Low-fat milk was used as received from market. These food products were spiked with diluted H. pylori culture to attain final bacterial concentrations of 105 and 106 cells mL−1. They were kept on shaker for 2 h at 37 °C. The prepared solutions were taken as analyte in the above-mentioned protocol and incubated with developed quenched system of FCD-Ab-GO. After that 1 mL of each sample was transferred to a cuvette and fluorescence spectrum was recorded and the recovery percentage was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H. pylori by ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of H. pylori was also conducted using conventional sandwich ELISA method [27]. Polystyrene (PS) 96-well microtiter plate was coated with 200 μL of 10 μg mL−1 of anti-H. pylori antibody for 16 h at 4 °C. Afterward, the unbound antibody was discarded, and the plate was thoroughly washed with Tween/PBS followed by addition 6% skimmed milk (250 μL) as blocking reagent and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. Then 200 μL of H. pylori solution with varying concentration of 0-107 cells mL−1 was transferred separately to each well of microtiter plate and incubated for same period. The zero concentration of bacteria was considered as a negative control. 200 μL of 1:1000 dilution of peroxidase-labelled antibody to H. Pylori was added to each well and incubated under same condition. Washings with Tween/PBS for thrice were done after each step of incubation except after HRP conjugation where only PBS was used. All incubation steps were dynamic as the plate was placed on a shaker at 100 rpm and each test was done at triplicate. Then 100 μL of 1X TMB/H2O2 was added to each well and kept for 10 min at room temperature to develop blue colour. The enzyme-substrate reaction was quenched by adding 100 μL of 0.5 M sulfuric acid and the optical density was measured in ELISA plate reader at 450 nm wavelength.

FCDs and GO were analyzed by UV/visible/NIR spectrometer using Lambda 25 software (PerkinElmer Inc. USA) in quartz crystal cuvettes of 1 cm path length. Surface morphology of synthesized FCDs was assessed using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) (TECNAI, FEI, USA) at an acceleration voltage of 200 KV. A thin film of FCDs was obtained using drop caste method onto 300 mesh copper grid coated with Holey carbon and air dried. Atomic Force Microscopic (AFM Innova®, Bruker) characterisation studies were carried out in tapping mode by using dried drop casted samples on silicon wafer. Particle size analysis of FCDs was done by Anton Paar Litesizer 500 Particle Analyzer using dilute solution. Fluorescence studies were carried out using PerkinElmer LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer where excitation and emission slit width were kept at 7.5 nm and 5 nm respectively with 650 Gain. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) studies were evaluated in Attenuated Total Reflection (ATR) mode using PerkinElmer Spectrum One FT-IR Spectrometer. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) of GO and FCD-Ab-GO were recorded using JEOL JSM-7800F Prime FESEM (Japan). Powdered sample of GO and FCD-Ab-GO were mounted on a metallic stub and coated with a thin layer of gold by spin coater (Bio-Rad Polaran Sputter, Model 50X). The Raman spectra were recorded using InVia Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) with accumulation time of 10 sec. 785 nm NIR laser light source (300 mW) with 1% laser power was used and the samples were focused using 50× long working distance objective lens. Elemental analysis was conducted by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (JEOL, JSM-7800F) and Optical density measurements of enzymatic activity were carried out on a BioTek (Epoch) microtitre ELISA plate reader at 450 nm wavelength. All the measurements were recorded at room temperature.

All the experiments were performed in triplicate and presented as mean value ± standard deviation. Microsoft Excel 365, Graphpad Prism 7 and Origin Pro 8.5 software were used for statistical analysis and for plotting the data in desired format. P ≤ 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The data of fluorescence studies was processed with FL WinLab™ software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterisation of FCDs and GO
ce:section.ce:para: Highly fluorescent functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) were prepared in microwave from citric acid as a carbon source and Tris as a dopant. Their morphological analysis was performed by HRTEM, which revealed that they are spherical in shape with an average diameter of around 4 nm and have uniform distribution (Fig. 1 a). AFM analysis (Fig. 1b) also confirms their spherical shape and height profile (inset) support the size distribution. DLS measurement in aqueous medium also verifies narrow particle size distribution with an average size of 3.79 nm (Fig. 1c). UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1d and e) were carried out to characterize the optical properties of FCDs. The absorption peak centred at 332 nm and a hump at 230 nm correspond to n-π* and π-π* transitions of CO and CC bonds, respectively, which were responsible for exhibiting fluorescence (FL). These results are in accordance with the previously reported literature [26,28]. When FCDs were excited with different wavelengths ranging from 290 to 380 nm, the emission peaks are red shifted and a symmetrical intense emission peak centred at 412 nm was observed with the excitation-maximum at 330 nm. Similar results were also observed by Zhang et al., 2016 [26]. Aqueous dispersion of FCDs is pale yellow in day light but displays strong blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1S). Major ATR-FTIR absorption bands for FCDs (Fig. 2 a (i)) were observed at 3319 cm−1 (C–OH str), 2953, 2886 cm−1 (aliphatic CH str), 1721 cm−1 (CO str for aliphatic carboxylic acid), 1665 cm−1 (CO for aliphatic amide), 1536 cm−1 (CC str), 1455 cm−1 (C–N), 1373 cm−1 (COO− symmetric str), 770 cm−1 (C–H out-of-plane bending vibration). Therefore, FCDs have carboxyl, hydroxyl and amide functionalities on their surface which is significant for easy dispersion in polar solvents and buffer solutions prerequisite for bioconjugation. EDX analysis (Fig. S2) indicated the elemental composition of FCDs and the major components were Carbon (48.9%) and Oxygen (42.9%) with a small amount of Nitrogen (8.2%) supporting the ATR-FTIR results.

ATR-FTIR spectrum of synthesized GO in transmission mode (Fig. 1f) reveals that the peaks at 3172, 1719 and 1617 cm−1 correspond to hydroxyl, carboxyl and ketonic functionalities, respectively. Moreover, the presence of some overlapped zones such as α-region (lactols, peroxides, dioxolanes, hydroxyls, epoxides) at 1039 cm−1 and γ-region (ethers, epoxide, peroxide, ketones, benzoquinonoes) at 1300-1500 cm−1 are attributed to oxidized graphene sheets [20]. Fig. 1g shows UV–visible absorption spectra of GO, which exhibited peaks in the UV region. A sharp peak at 242 nm shows usual π-π* transition of aromatic C–C bonds while a small shoulder at 300 nm reflects n-π* transition of CO bonds. These results are in agreement with the reported study [29]. The Raman spectrum of GO exhibits well-documented strong G-band at 1593 cm−1 and a relatively weak D-band at 1353 cm−1 (Fig. S3). The G-band implies the presence of sp2 carbon whereas the D-band is an indication of disorder, which generally arises from certain defects. The peak intensity ratio of D and G bands (I
D
/I
G ) is 0.89 which reflects the structural imperfection caused by the oxygen containing functional groups on the carbon basal plane of GO [23].

EDC and NHS are widely used as coupling agents which enhance the coupling efficiency between carboxyl and amino groups. The synthesized FCDs with carboxyl groups on surface activated by EDC and NHS, could conjugate with amino functionalities of antibodies to form FCD-Ab bioconjugates [30,31]. ATR-FTIR analysis of FCD-Ab (Fig. 2a (ii)) revealed that absorption band of –COOH at 1721 cm−1 reduced noticeably with shifted position at 1728 cm−1, indicating covalent coupling between the carboxyl functionalities of FCDs and the amino groups present in lysin moiety of antibodies. A new band appeared at 3341 cm−1 (Fig. 2a) attributed to amine groups of Abs, confirming the covalent attachment of Abs on the FCDs surface. Immobilization of Abs caused a slight blue shift of the emission peak of FCD-Ab with a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2b) and a change in zeta potential value from −26 mV to −17 mV, confirming the successful bioconjugation [32].

FCD-Ab emits blue fluorescence when illuminated with 330 nm monochromatic light. Energy transfer from FCD-Ab to GO was explored by measuring the fluorescence quenching of FCDs upon addition of GO. Fig. 2c shows the fluorescence spectra of FCD-Ab when GO was conjugated to form FCD-Ab-GO complexes with increasing concentrations. As the concentration of GO increases from 0 to 60 μg mL−1, the fluorescence signal is found to decrease gradually. After attaining an optimal concentration of 50 μg mL−1, there is no further reduction in fluorescence intensity even at a higher concentration of GO. In addition, all the solutions containing different concentrations of GO (0–60 μg mL−1) are transparent and well dispersed as shown in Fig. S4 suggesting absence of light scattering phenomenon. Therefore, the result confirms that the fluorescence of FCD-Ab can be successfully quenched by GO. The Stern-Volmer plot as shown in Fig. 2d, displayed a nonlinear increase with increasing GO concentration from 0 to 50 μg mL−1, indicating the presence of both static and dynamic quenching [23]. As a result of conjugation, FCD-Ab was brought close to GO surface and fluorescence quenching was observed.

To estimate the optimum time for maximum interaction of GO with FCD-Ab, reaction mixtures were incubated for different time periods ranging from 5 to 30 min and subsequently fluorescence intensity was measured (Fig. S5). The optimum incubation time was taken to be 20 min with 89% quenching efficiency at 50 μg mL−1 of GO concentration. Hence, these conditions were used for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis of GO and FCD-Ab-GO complex
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM image of GO showed layered nanosheet structure with the thickness around 15 nm (Fig. 2e). The surface morphology of GO is also studied after interacting with FCD-Ab conjugate to verify the formation of FCD-Ab-GO complex. As shown in Fig. 2f, many small, uniformly distributed globules were observed on the surface of GO nanosheet confirming the attachment of FCD-Ab on GO. Thus, the FESEM analysis clearly demonstrates that FCD-Abs are adsorbed on the surface of GO nanosheet leading to the fluorescence quenching. Similar observation was also reported by Lu et al. [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of FRET based immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: FCDs as a fluoroprobe and GO as a quencher are employed in the developed FRET immunosensor instead of two different dyes which are generally used in FRET based sensors for sensing. The synthesized GO nanosheet are hydrophilic in nature due to the presence of oxygen containing functional groups (OH, COOH, epoxy) and show good dispersibility in polar solvent which is crucial for maximum interaction with FCDs and Abs resulting in successful bioconjugation. Moreover, 2D planar surface area of GO provides a large number of bonding sites with effective quenching distance extendable to 30 nm [28]. In detection procedure when the target analyte is absent, the conjugates, FCD-Ab, were adsorbed on a GO surface via π-π stacking interaction as well as hydrogen bonding between COOH/OH groups of GO and the amine groups of antibodies [22,33]. Therese interactions help in holding the fluorescence indicator FCDs in close proximity to the surface of GO leading to the substantial fluorescence quenching of FCD-Abs via FRET from FCD-Abs to GO [34]. But in the presence of analyte, H. pylori, FCD-Ab can recognize the target with high specificity through the binding of antibodies to the cell membrane of pathogen to form an immunocomplex. In addition, affinity of antibodies towards its antigen causes a lesser binding ability towards GO as a result FCDs get far away from GO surface facilitating interruption of energy transfer between doner (FCDs) and acceptor (GO) thus leads to the recovery of fluorescence (Scheme 1). Moreover, GO does not have any fluorescence of its own and therefore, makes no contribution towards measured fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the developed immunosensor is highly sensitive to H. pylori and the target analyte preferably binds to antibodies rather than the surface of GO nanosheet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and selectivity study of the FRET immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the developed immunosensor was further evaluated using various non-target pathogens such as E. coli, S. sonnei, S. aureus and S. typhimurium and the results are shown in Fig. 4 a. FCD-Ab-GO complex was separately incubated with 107 cells mL−1 concentration of H. pylori and the above-mentioned cross-reactive bacteria followed by measuring the respective fluorescence intensity at 412 nm. It was observed that the addition of non-specific pathogens does not enhance the emission intensity significantly. However, upon addition of H. pylori, a dramatic increase in the restored fluorescence was recorded (p = 0.0001) owing to strong and specific binding of antibody with its targeted pathogen. This antibody does not display any affinity towards other non-specific bacteria due to weak binding interactions between antibody and dissimilar antigens. As a result, the π-π stacking interactions between FCD-Ab and GO was maintained which leads to disruption of fluorescence recovery. To evaluate the selectivity of FCD-Ab-GO complex, further testing was carried out with mixed pool of bacteria. Detection of H. pylori in presence of S. typhimurium was conducted where a mixture of equi-concentration of both the bacteria (0.5 × 107 cells mL−1 each) in 1X PBS was taken as analyte and incubated with FCD-Ab-GO complex. The fluorescence intensity obtained for mixed bacteria was almost similar to that of pure H. pylori (Fig. 4a) whereas pure S. typhimurium gave the low fluorescence response comparable to blank control without target. The results confirm that the developed immunosensor has high specificity and can selectively recognize the target pathogen even in the presence of large concentration of cross-reactants.

Though, the route of H. pylori infection is not yet been clearly known but one of the commonly acknowledged hypotheses is that food can play a vital role in transmission of the organism. Moreover, H. pylori is considered as a food-borne pathogen because of its epidemiological and microbial characteristics [12,13]. High prevalence of H. pylori in complex food matrix such as milk, vegetables, ready-to-eat salad, and foods of animal origin are because of poor hygiene and microbial contamination during post-processing, packaging and transportation. Milk is one of the major possible sources of H. pylori transmission due to the presence of urea in it which helps the microbe to survive for longer time [2,40]. Furthermore, the pylori infection dose is low for human, thus, even a small number of bacteria in food can cause a potential health hazard for consumers.

Feasibility and reliability validation of the developed immunosensor was tested using real food samples of different texture such as lettuce, fenugreek leaves, milk and minced meat. These samples, free of any bacterial contaminant, were spiked with two different concentrations of H. pylori 105 and 106 cells mL−1. These foods are specially chosen due to high prevalence of H. pylori as reported by Quaglia et al. [2]. The spikes samples were analyzed in triplicate using FCD-Ab-GO complex and the recovered fluorescence were recorded (Fig. 4b). A negligible interference of food matrix was observed for all samples except for minced meat where signal to noise ratio was relatively low which may be due to the presence of proteinaceous moieties in meat, inhibiting Ab-Ag interactions to certain extent. The recovery rates were calculated for these samples based on the calibration curve obtained with H. pylori in PBS solution and the results are summarized in Table 2 . Appreciable recovery in the range of 90–106% (n = 3) was observed suggesting the potential practical utility of the developed FRET immunosensor for evaluating the microbial contamination in food and thus ensuring the general safety of the consumable food items. In the present study although the designed FRET platform provides a sensitive detection of whole cell H. pylori, a microbe of concern, but can also be used as a universal platform for analysing other organism with requisite biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H. pylori using sandwich ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of FRET immunosensor was compared with conventional sandwich ELISA assay. ELISA was conducted in Polystyrene (PS) 96-well microtiter plate which was coated with anti-H. pylori antibody (primary antibody) and subsequently treated with skimmed milk to block the vacant sites. The binding of antibodies occurs through noncovalent interaction (physical adsorption) and prone to leached out with extensive washing step during assay. This may lead to nonspecific adsorption of HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies on to the surface of PS plate which results into the generation of undesired signals. Additionally, it was observed that the ELISA assay required minimum 103 cells mL−1 concentration of pathogen to obtain marginal increase of optical density (OD) whereas concentration lesser than 103 cells mL−1 produced signal comparable to that of negative control showcasing leaser sensitivity of the assay (Fig. S6). Therefore, the assay is not sufficiently sensitive to detect the bacteria at lower level (<103 cells mL−1) and the linear range for analytical detection was found to be 104 -107 cells mL−1 with LOD of 104 cells mL−1 whereas the LOD of FRET based immunosensor was quite low (10 cells mL−1, Sec. 3.6). Thus, the result confirms the advantages of the developed FRET immunosensor for the sensitive and efficient detection of H. pylori.

A novel and sensitive FRET based immunosensor was developed for the quantitative detection of whole cell H. pylori using functionalized carbon dots and graphene oxide. Highly fluorescent functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) were synthesized from citric acid and Tris with high quantum yield due to the presence of graphitic nitrogen, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface. As Tris was used in trace amount, majority of carboxyl groups of citric acid were retained on the surface of FCDs which were exploited for successful bioconjugation of antibodies using EDC/NHS chemistry. The FCD-Ab conjugates exhibit strong fluorescence and good dispersion stability. FESEM image showed successful attachment of FCD-Ab onto the surface of GO nanosheet, and subsequent fluorescence quenching was confirmed by fluorescence spectra. In presence of target pathogen, the intensity of recovered fluorescence directly corelates with increasing concentration of bacteria. Moreover, high quenching efficiency of GO enhances the sensitivity of the developed FRET immunosensor which in turn provides a wide range of detection of H. pylori with LOD of 10 cells mL−1. The developed FRET immunosensor was found to be specific and selective to target pathogen and also exhibit good reproducibility. Measurement of H. pylori in real food samples with appreciable recovery demonstrates the scope for practical applications of this approach for risk assessment in food and beverage industries and testing laboratories for detecting target bacteria. Though the current study deals with specific and sensitive detection of H. pylori, the horizon can be further extended for the detection of other pathogens as well.

Sruti Chattopadhyay: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition (Women Scientist Programme), Investigation, Methodology, Project Administration, Validation, Writing (original draft), Review and Editing.
Meenakshi Choudhary: Methodology, Review and Editing.
Harpal Singh: Review and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Sruti Chattopadhyay acknowledges financial support provided by Department of Health Research (DHR), Govt. of India (12013/15/2019-HR). We thank Mohd Anees for his help in scientific discussion.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2022.114801.
